substances.name: bleach (sodium hypochlorite)
first_aid: Call Poison Control immediately: 1-800-222-1222 (US) or your regional center.
Do NOT induce vomiting — bleach is caustic and causes additional burns on the way back up.
If swallowed: rinse mouth with water, then give small sips of water or milk (4–8 oz adults, 2–4 oz children) to dilute.
If on skin or eyes: flush with large amounts of cool running water for 15–20 minutes.
If inhaled fumes: move to fresh air immediately; loosen tight clothing.
Remove contaminated clothing and wash skin thoroughly with soap and water.
Monitor breathing; if difficulty breathing develops, call 911.

substances.name: ammonia
first_aid: Call Poison Control immediately: 1-800-222-1222 (US) or your regional center.
Do NOT induce vomiting — ammonia is corrosive and causes additional airway and esophageal burns.
NEVER mix ammonia with bleach — produces toxic chloramine gas; evacuate if this occurred.
If swallowed: rinse mouth, give small sips of water or milk (4–8 oz adults, 2–4 oz children).
If on skin: remove contaminated clothing; flush with copious running water for 15–20 minutes.
If in eyes: flush with clean water for at least 20 minutes, holding eyelids open.
If inhaled: move to fresh air immediately; call 911 if breathing is labored or person is unconscious.

substances.name: medication overdose (general)
first_aid: Call 911 or Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 immediately — do NOT wait for symptoms.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless explicitly instructed by Poison Control or a clinician.
Note the medication name, dose, and estimated quantity taken; have this ready for responders.
If the person is unconscious and breathing: place in recovery position (on their side) to protect the airway.
If the person stops breathing: begin CPR at 100–120 compressions/min and call 911 if not already done.
Do not leave the person alone.
Save the pill bottle or packaging to give to emergency responders.

substances.name: opioids (heroin, fentanyl, prescription opioids)
first_aid: Call 911 immediately — opioid overdose is rapidly fatal without intervention.
Administer naloxone (Narcan) if available: one spray into one nostril (or inject per kit instructions); repeat in 2–3 minutes if no response.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
If unconscious and not breathing normally: begin rescue breathing (1 breath every 5 seconds) or full CPR.
Place in recovery position if breathing but unconscious to prevent choking on vomit.
Stay until EMS arrives — naloxone wears off in 30–90 minutes and person can re-sedate.
Good Samaritan laws protect callers in most US states — call 911 without fear.
substances.antidote: naloxone (Narcan) — reverses opioid effects; available without prescription at many pharmacies

substances.name: acetaminophen (Tylenol / paracetamol)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 or 911 immediately — liver damage is delayed 24–72 hours and may not be apparent.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
Note exact dose taken, timing, and any other substances consumed.
Hospital treatment with N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is highly effective if given early — time is critical.
Do not give any additional medications without medical guidance.
Even if the person feels well, immediate evaluation is essential because symptoms appear late.
substances.antidote: N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — administered in hospital; most effective within 8–10 hours of ingestion

substances.name: button battery (disc battery)
first_aid: Call 911 or go to the emergency department IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for symptoms.
Do NOT induce vomiting — can worsen damage and delay definitive care.
Do NOT eat or drink anything (keep NPO for potential surgery).
If a button battery is swallowed and the child is over 1 year old, give 2 teaspoons of honey every 10 minutes en route to the ER ONLY if advised by Poison Control — honey may slow tissue damage.
Do NOT give honey to infants under 12 months.
Time is critical: a 20mm lithium battery can burn through the esophagus within 2 hours.
If battery is lodged in the ear or nose: do not attempt removal at home — go to the ED immediately.
substances.antidote: Surgical or endoscopic removal — emergency procedure; honey may reduce injury in transit

substances.name: antifreeze (ethylene glycol)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 or 911 immediately.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
Note the product name, amount ingested, and time of ingestion.
Antifreeze tastes sweet — do not underestimate ingestion in children or pets.
Hospital treatment with fomepizole or ethanol is required; early treatment prevents kidney failure.
Keep the person still and calm while awaiting emergency services.
If on skin: remove contaminated clothing and wash with soap and water.
substances.antidote: Fomepizole (4-MP) or ethanol — administered in hospital to block toxic metabolism

substances.name: carbon monoxide
first_aid: Get everyone — including yourself — out of the building immediately; do not re-enter.
Call 911 from outside or from a neighbor's home.
Do not induce vomiting (not applicable — CO is inhaled, not ingested).
Lie down if dizzy or unconscious — keeping the airway open is the priority.
If the person is unconscious and not breathing: begin CPR at 100–120 compressions/min; call 911.
High-flow oxygen is the primary treatment — paramedics will administer it via mask.
Do not re-enter the building until emergency services clear it.
substances.antidote: High-flow oxygen (100%) — administered by EMS; hyperbaric oxygen for severe cases

substances.name: alcohol (ethanol — acute poisoning)
first_aid: Call 911 if the person is unconscious, not responding, breathing fewer than 8 breaths/min, or having seizures.
Do NOT induce vomiting — risk of aspiration is high; unconscious persons can choke.
Place an unconscious but breathing person in the recovery position (on their side) to prevent choking.
Keep the person warm — alcohol causes heat loss and hypothermia.
Do not give coffee or water to 'sober them up' — time and medical support are the only treatments.
Stay with the person and monitor breathing continuously.
Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 if concerned about quantity consumed.

substances.name: household cleaner (multi-surface, drain cleaner, oven cleaner)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 immediately and follow their specific instructions.
Do NOT induce vomiting — caustic cleaners cause severe burns to the mouth, throat, and esophagus on the way back up.
For skin contact: remove contaminated clothing and flush affected area with cool running water for 15–20 minutes.
For eye contact: flush eyes with clean water for at least 20 minutes, holding eyelids open; seek eye care evaluation.
If swallowed: rinse mouth with water; give small sips of water only if instructed by Poison Control.
If inhaling fumes: move to fresh air; call 911 if breathing is difficult.
Save the container or take a photo of the label to assist medical staff.

substances.name: pesticide (organophosphate, carbamate, rodenticide)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 and 911 immediately — pesticide poisoning can progress quickly.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
Identify the specific product if possible — different pesticide classes require different treatments.
For skin contact: remove contaminated clothing using gloves; flush skin with soap and large amounts of water.
For eye contact: flush with clean water for 15–20 minutes.
If inhaled: move person to fresh air; loosen restrictive clothing; administer CPR if not breathing.
Warning signs of organophosphate poisoning: excessive salivation, tearing, small pupils, muscle twitching — report these immediately to responders.
substances.antidote: Atropine and pralidoxime for organophosphates — administered in hospital; vitamin K1 for anticoagulant rodenticides

substances.name: unknown plant or berry
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 immediately — do not wait for symptoms.
Do NOT induce vomiting unless Poison Control specifically directs you to.
Try to identify the plant: take a photo or collect a sample (with leaves, berries, or flowers if possible).
Note how much was eaten, which part of the plant, and when.
Rinse mouth with water; do not eat or drink more until cleared by Poison Control.
Watch for: rash, swelling of mouth/throat, drooling, nausea, vomiting, confusion, or irregular heartbeat.
Call 911 immediately if the person has difficulty breathing, seizures, or loses consciousness.

substances.name: iron supplements (acute iron toxicity)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 and 911 immediately — iron toxicity is especially dangerous in children.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
Note the number of tablets missing and the iron content per tablet (mg elemental iron).
Symptoms can appear delayed 6–24 hours — absence of early symptoms does not mean safety.
Hospital treatment with deferoxamine chelation is required for significant ingestion.
Keep the bottle and label to give to emergency responders.
substances.antidote: Deferoxamine — administered in hospital for iron chelation

substances.name: lead (paint chips, dust, or water)
first_aid: Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 — for acute exposure, prioritize decontamination.
Do NOT induce vomiting.
Remove the child or person from the source of exposure immediately.
Wash hands, face, and any exposed skin thoroughly with soap and water.
For ingested paint chips: do NOT induce vomiting; seek medical evaluation for blood lead testing.
Arrange for the source (old paint, pipes) to be properly remediated by a certified professional.
Blood lead testing is necessary to assess exposure level — symptoms are often delayed or subtle.
substances.antidote: Chelation therapy (succimer/DMSA or EDTA) — prescribed by physician based on blood lead level
